title: practical english usage - Part 289
content:
(     ... he's nearly-humam) Our  cat understands everything - he's almost human.

statements less definite. Nearly is not used like this.

We   can use almost to mean 'similar to, but not exactly the same', and to make 2 other     meanings not I've very/pretty nearly finished, (     . . .-very almost.. .) Very and pretty can be used with nearly but not almost.

It's almost ten o'clock. (= perhaps 9.57) It's nearly ten o'clock. (= perhaps 9.45) Sometimes almost is a little 'nearer' than nearly. Compare: There were almost/nearly a thousand people there.

I've almost/nearly finished.

measurement or counting. Nearly is less common in American English.

Almost and nearly can both express ideas connected with progress, 1  progress,      measurement          and   counting 366 almost        and   nearly;      practically          ______ For more about let, ►    225, 512.

She wouldn't let me in. I've been let down.

Let can be used with adverb particles; passives are possible in this case.

I wasn't allowed to pay for the drinks, (     I wasn't let...) Let is not usually used in the passive.

Let me buy you a drink, (friendly and informal) Please allow me to buy you a drink, (polite and formal) without to. Compare: Let is the least formal of these three words, and is followed by object + infinitive 2  let She wouldn't allow me in. Emily isn't allowed out at night.

Allow, but not permit, can be used with adverb particles.

(          It is-not-allowed-to-smoke-...) It is not permitted to smoke in the kitchen.

The  passive structure with it is only possible with permit.

vocabulary •  368   along ►    595 already,       still    and   let ►    520 aloud      and   loudly Come along (= Come with me) or walking along (= walking on one’ s way).

Note the special use of along as an adverb particle in expressions like right through the meal not all through the Journey (    all along the journey) not through the centuries (     along the centuries) To  talk about periods or activities, we  prefer through.

His office is along the corridor.

I saw her running along the road.

that refer to things with a long thin shape.

The  preposition along is used before nouns like road, river, corridor, line: words 368 along The only green thing was a lone/solitary pine tree.

instead; lone is rather literary.

Alone is not used before a noun (►   183.3). Lone and solitary can be used After her husband died, she was all alone.

Alone can be emphasised by all.

But after a few days I start getting lonely/lonesome.

I like to be alone for short periods.

means ‘ alone and unhappy because of it’ . Compare: Alone means ‘ without others around’ . Lonely (and informal AmE   lonesome) 367 alone,      lon   ely,    lonesome          and   lone    _____ She's practically never at home.

I've practically finished.  Jake is practically like a father to me.

Practically can be used in the same way as almost.

He's been almost everywhere. He eats almost anything.

She likes almost everybody.   Almost anybody can do this job.

more common than nearly before anybody/-one/-thing/-where.

We   also prefer almost before everybody/-one/-thing/-where, and almost is much 4 everybody,           everything,           anybody,         anything,          etc Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  369   also, as well and too I'm alternately happy and depressed.

We spend alternate weekends at our country cottage.

Alternate(ly) means 'first one and then the other’ , 'in turns’ .

370 alternate(ly)           and   alternative(ly) For as well as, ►    382.

For also and even. ►    452.3.

For also, as well, too and either in negative clauses, ►    227.

I, too, have experienced despair.

In a formal or literary style, too can be placed directly after the subject.

4 too   in  a  formal     style but not More formal equivalents are So am I (►   309) or I am too (           I-also).

’ I'm going home.’ ’ Me too.’ In very informal speech, we  often use Me too as a short answer.

’ I've got a headache.’ ’ I have too.’ (More natural than I also have.) ’ She's nice.’ ’ Her sister is as well.’ (More natural than Her sister is also.) Give me some bread as well, please. (More natural than Also give me ...) As well and too are used in imperatives and short answers, but not usually also.
